task: I’m dealing with a billing disagreement on a delivery, and I want to answer it carefully without making assumptions. What’s the best way to line up the paperwork and any other proof so I can sort out what actually happened before I respond?

turns.agent_response:
A good way to handle it is to separate **what is documented** from **what is inferred**.

### 1) Build a simple timeline
List the delivery from start to finish in order:
- order date
- invoice date
- ship date
- delivery date
- any customer complaint date

### 2) Gather the core documents
Pull together:
- purchase order / sales order
- invoice
- packing slip
- bill of lading / delivery receipt
- carrier tracking or proof of delivery
- any signed delivery paperwork
- emails, texts, or notes tied to the issue

### 3) Compare the key details side by side
Check whether these match across documents:
- item numbers / descriptions
- quantities
- pricing / charges
- delivery address
- delivery date/time
- signature or receiver name
- any damage, shortage, or exception notes

A simple table helps:

| Detail | Paperwork says | Other proof says | Match? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quantity | 10 | 10 | Yes |
| Delivery date | 5/12 | 5/12 | Yes |
| Signature | J. Smith | POD shows J. Smith | Yes |

### 4) Mark facts vs. questions
Use two buckets:
- **Confirmed facts:** supported by documents or records
- **Open questions:** things not yet proven

This keeps you from responding based on memory or assumption.

### 5) Look for the strongest proof first
Usually the order of reliability is:
1. signed proof of delivery
2. carrier tracking / GPS / scan records
3. invoice / packing slip
4. internal notes or emails
5. recollections

### 6) Check for the most common mismatch points
Disputes often come down to:
- wrong quantity
- wrong item
- freight/accessorial charge
- delivery to the wrong location
- partial delivery
- customer signed without noting an exception

### 7) Draft your response only from verified points
A safe structure is:
- acknowledge the concern
- state what records show
- note what is still being checked, if anything
- avoid saying “you must have…” or “it was probably…”

Example:
> “I reviewed the invoice, packing slip, and delivery receipt. The records show 8 units delivered on 5/12 to the signed receiving location. I’m still checking one charge detail and will follow up once confirmed.”

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page dispute checklist** you can reuse for future billing issues.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right approach. The timeline plus side-by-side comparison is what I’d use too. The part I like most is separating confirmed facts from open questions — that keeps you from talking yourself into a story before the paperwork backs it up.

I’d probably add a couple things:
- pull the original order too, not just the invoice
- if there’s damage involved, get the photos with date stamps if you have them
- check who signed and whether there was any exception noted at delivery
- if the charge is freight-related, look at the rate sheet or terms that were in effect

That sample response is solid. Straight, no guessing, and it leaves room to keep checking without sounding evasive. That’s usually the cleanest way to handle it.
